# UI Engineering

## Overview

Build production-quality user interfaces that are accessible, performant, and visually polished. The goal is UI that looks like it was built by a design-aware engineer at a top company — not like it was generated by an AI. This means real design system adherence, proper accessibility, thoughtful interaction patterns, and no generic "AI aesthetic."

This skill applies across all frontend frameworks and vanilla web. For React-specific patterns (hooks, JSX composition, React Query, context API), also invoke the `react-ui` skill.

## When to Use

- Building new UI components or pages
- Modifying existing user-facing interfaces
- Implementing responsive layouts
- Adding interactivity or state management
- Fixing visual or UX issues

## Component Architecture

### File Structure

Colocate everything related to a component:

```
src/components/
  TaskList/
    TaskList.[ext]        # Component implementation
    TaskList.test.[ext]   # Tests
    task-list.css         # Component-scoped styles (if not using utility CSS)
    types.[ext]           # Component-specific types (if needed)
```

### Component Principles

**Prefer composition over configuration:**

Build small, focused components that compose together rather than large, highly-configured components. Deep prop/attribute lists signal a component doing too much.

**Keep components focused:**

A component should do one thing well. If a component handles data fetching, rendering, and complex interactions simultaneously, split it into container and presentation layers.

**Separate data fetching from presentation:**

- **Container layer**: Fetches data, handles loading/error/empty states, passes clean data down.
- **Presentation layer**: Receives data via props/attributes and renders it. Has no knowledge of how data was obtained.

This separation makes presentation components testable in isolation and reusable across data sources.

**Size limit:** If a component exceeds ~200 lines, it is doing too much. Split it.

## State Management

Choose the simplest approach that satisfies the requirement:

```
Component-local state        → UI-only state (open/closed, hover, active tab)
Lifted/shared state          → Shared between 2–3 sibling components
URL state (query params)     → Filters, pagination, shareable UI state
Server/remote state cache    → Remote data with mutation and caching
Global application store     → Complex client state shared across the app
```

**Avoid passing state through components that don't use it.** If more than 2–3 component levels separate state from consumer, use a different sharing mechanism.

## Design System Adherence

### Avoid the AI Aesthetic

AI-generated UI has recognizable patterns. Avoid all of them:

| AI Default | Production Quality |
|---|---|
| Purple/indigo everything | Use the project's actual color palette |
| Excessive gradients | Flat or subtle gradients matching the design system |
| Rounded everything | Consistent border-radius from the design system |
| Generic hero sections | Content-first layouts |
| Lorem ipsum-style copy | Realistic placeholder content |
| Oversized padding everywhere | Consistent spacing scale |
| Stock card grids | Purpose-driven layouts |
| Shadow-heavy design | Subtle or no shadows unless the design system specifies |

### Spacing and Layout

Use a consistent spacing scale. Don't invent values:

```css
/* Use the project scale: e.g. 0.25rem increments */
padding: 1rem;        /* ✓ On the scale */
gap: 0.75rem;         /* ✓ On the scale */
padding: 13px;        /* ✗ Not on any scale */
margin-top: 2.3rem;   /* ✗ Not on any scale */
```

Avoid inline styles. Use utility classes, CSS custom properties, or design tokens.

### Typography

Respect the type hierarchy:

```
h1 → Page title (one per page)
h2 → Section title
h3 → Subsection title
body → Default text
small → Secondary/helper text
```

Don't skip heading levels. Don't use heading styles on non-heading content.

### Color

- Use semantic color tokens: `text-primary`, `bg-surface`, `border-default` — not raw hex values
- Ensure sufficient contrast (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text — WCAG 2.1 AA)
- Don't rely solely on color to convey information; pair with icons, text, or patterns

## Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA)

Every component must meet these standards.

For final WCAG 2.1 AA gating and verification, invoke the `ui-accessibility` skill and apply checks from `references/accessibility-checklist.md`.

### Keyboard Navigation

Every interactive element must be reachable and operable with a keyboard:

```html
<!-- ✓ Natively focusable -->
<button>Click me</button>
<a href="/page">Navigate</a>
<input type="text" />

<!-- ✗ Not keyboard accessible -->
<div onclick="handleClick()">Click me</div>

<!-- ✓ If a non-interactive element must be interactive -->
<div role="button" tabindex="0">Click me</div>
```

Use native semantic elements (`<button>`, `<a>`, `<input>`, `<select>`) wherever possible. They come with keyboard behavior built in.

### ARIA Labels

```html
<!-- Interactive elements without visible text need labels -->
<button aria-label="Close dialog">✕</button>

<!-- Form inputs need associated labels -->
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input id="email" type="email" />

<!-- Or aria-label when no visible label is present -->
<input aria-label="Search tasks" type="search" />
```

### Focus Management

Move focus when content changes significantly:

- Opening a modal: move focus to the first interactive element or the modal itself.
- Closing a modal: return focus to the trigger element.
- Route changes: move focus to the page heading or main landmark.

Trap focus inside modals and dialogs while they are open.

### Meaningful Empty and Error States

Never show blank screens. Every list or content area needs three states handled:

- **Loading**: skeleton or spinner with `aria-busy="true"` and a descriptive label.
- **Empty**: informative message and a primary action to resolve the empty state.
- **Error**: clear message, reason where possible, and a recovery action (retry, reload).

## Responsive Design

Design mobile-first, then expand to larger breakpoints:

```
Base styles        → Mobile (320px+)
sm: breakpoint     → Tablet (640px+)
md: breakpoint     → Small desktop (768px+)
lg: breakpoint     → Desktop (1024px+)
xl: breakpoint     → Wide (1280px+)
```

Test at: 320px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px.

Avoid fixed widths that break at small viewports. Use `max-width` with responsive padding instead.

## Loading and Perceived Performance

- Use **skeleton loading** for content areas — not full-page spinners.
- Apply **optimistic updates** for user actions where the expected success rate is high (toggle, like, delete).
- On optimistic update failure, roll back state and surface a clear error.
- Lazy-load routes and heavy components to reduce initial bundle size.
- Preload critical assets (fonts, above-the-fold images) to reduce layout shift (CLS).

## Invocation Example

Quick example to run when finishing a UI change (framework-agnostic):

- Run the app locally (e.g., `npm start` or framework dev server).
- Automated checks: `npx axe-core ./path/to/page-or-static.html` and/or `npx pa11y http://localhost:3000/route`.
- Manual checks: keyboard-only navigation, screen reader smoke test, and zoom to 200%.
- Final gating: invoke the `ui-accessibility` skill and attach the checklist report from `references/accessibility-checklist.md`.

- Agent invocation (one-liner): Invoke `ui-accessibility` attaching `axe-report.json`, `pa11y-report.html`, and `manual-checks.md` as artifacts.

## Common Rationalizations

| Rationalization | Reality |
|---|---|
| "Accessibility is a nice-to-have" | It's a legal requirement in many jurisdictions and an engineering quality standard. |
| "We'll make it responsive later" | Retrofitting responsive design is 3x harder than building it from the start. |
| "The design isn't final, so I'll skip styling" | Use the design system defaults. Unstyled UI creates a broken first impression for reviewers. |
| "This is just a prototype" | Prototypes become production code. Build the foundation right. |
| "The AI aesthetic is fine for now" | It signals low quality. Use the project's actual design system from the start. |

## Red Flags

- Components with more than 200 lines
- Inline styles or arbitrary pixel values not on the spacing scale
- Missing loading, error, or empty states
- Interactive elements unreachable by keyboard
- Color as the sole indicator of state (red/green without text or icons)
- Generic "AI look" (purple gradients, oversized cards, stock layouts)
- Heading levels skipped or used for visual styling only

## Verification

After building UI:

- [ ] Component renders without console errors
- [ ] All interactive elements are keyboard accessible (Tab through the page)
- [ ] Screen reader can convey the page's content and structure
- [ ] Responsive: works at 320px, 768px, 1024px, 1440px
- [ ] Loading, error, and empty states all handled
- [ ] Follows the project's design system (spacing, colors, typography)
- [ ] No accessibility warnings in dev tools or axe-core

## Output Requirements

For each finding or completed build session provide:

- **Location** — Component name and file path.
- **Category** — Accessibility, state management, design system, component architecture, responsive design, or performance.
- **Severity** — Critical (blocks users or violates WCAG), Important (degrades UX or violates design system), Suggestion (improvement opportunity).
- **Evidence** — What was observed or what the code does currently.
- **Fix** — Minimal corrected code example or specific action to take.

When delivering built UI, include:
- Summary of components created or changed.
- Verification checklist results.
- Any accessibility or design system deviations that require design input.
